name: NIH-Prompt-Approach
description: NIH Research Strategy - Approach subsection with preliminary studies, research design, timeline

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the APPROACH subsection of the Research Strategy for an NIH grant proposal. INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for all methodological content, prior results, and experimental details. - {{grant_info}}: The specific NIH grant opportunity description, including mechanism (R01, R21, R03), focus area, and any specific methodological requirements. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Approach subsection of the Research Strategy. Do NOT generate Specific Aims, Significance, Innovation, Budget, Data Management Plan, Human Subjects sections, Environment, Biographical Sketches, Project Summary, or any other proposal component. Those are handled separately. SECTION STRUCTURE: C. APPROACH C.1 PRELIMINARY STUDIES / PROGRESS REPORT (1-2 pages) For new applications: - Summarize the research team's prior work from {{content}} that establishes expertise and feasibility - Present pilot data, preliminary results, or proof-of-concept findings from {{content}} - For each preliminary result, explicitly connect it to a specific aim of the proposed research - Reference the team's own publications from {{content}} - If {{content}} includes quantitative results (performance metrics, effect sizes, measurement accuracies), present them with appropriate context - Describe technical expertise demonstrated by prior work For renewals (if {{content}} indicates prior NIH support): - Summarize major accomplishments from the prior period - Describe progress toward each previous aim - Present publications resulting from prior support - Describe new preliminary data supporting the renewal C.2 RESEARCH DESIGN AND METHODS For EACH specific aim (identify aims from the research objectives in {{content}} — typically 2-4 aims): For each aim, cover ALL of these subsections: a. RATIONALE - Why this aim is scientifically important - How it builds on preliminary data and prior work - How it connects to other aims b. STUDY DESIGN OVERVIEW - High-level description of the experimental or study design - Key variables (independent, dependent, covariates) - If applicable: study population, sample size with power justification, inclusion/exclusion criteria c. METHODS AND PROCEDURES - Detailed description of methods grounded in {{content}} - Specific protocols, instruments, assays, or analytical approaches - Use the researcher's own terminology and methods from {{content}} - For computational/analytical methods: describe algorithms, software, validation approaches as described in {{content}} - Do NOT assume AI/ML methods unless {{content}} describes them - For experimental methods: describe procedures, controls, replication strategy - For clinical methods: describe assessments, data collection, quality control - Address rigor and reproducibility: * Scientific rigor: blinding, randomization, controls, replication as appropriate * Biological variables: sex as a biological variable if applicable * Authentication of key biological resources if applicable d. DATA ANALYSIS - Statistical or analytical plan for this aim - Primary and secondary analyses - Multiple comparison corrections if applicable - Software to be used - Match the analysis approach to the specific methods and data types in {{content}} e. EXPECTED OUTCOMES - What results are anticipated if the hypothesis is supported - What results would be expected if the hypothesis is not supported - How results will be interpreted in either case - How outcomes feed into subsequent aims f. POTENTIAL PROBLEMS AND ALTERNATIVE STRATEGIES - Identify 2-3 specific, realistic problems that could arise - For each problem, provide a concrete alternative strategy - Format: "Potential Problem: [specific issue] Alternative Strategy: [specific solution and why it will work]" - Draw from challenges the researcher has encountered or anticipated in {{content}} C.3 TIMELINE AND MILESTONES Create a timeline table appropriate to the grant mechanism: - R01: 5-year timeline, organized by year - R21: 2-year timeline, organized by 6-month periods - R03: 2-year timeline, organized by 6-month periods Format as a table with columns for: Time Period | Aim 1 Activities | Aim 2 Activities | [Aim 3] | Key Milestones Include major milestones: - Regulatory approvals (IRB, IACUC) if applicable - Data collection/generation completion points - Analysis completion points - Manuscript submission targets - Go/no-go decision points between phases WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for expert peer reviewers in the specific field described in {{content}} - The Approach is the most heavily weighted section in NIH review — it must demonstrate that the research plan is feasible, rigorous, and well-reasoned - Use precise technical terminology from {{content}} - Be specific about methods: name specific assays, instruments, statistical tests, software — drawn from {{content}} - Do NOT reframe the research as artificial intelligence or machine learning unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML methods - Do NOT fabricate preliminary data, methods, or results not in {{content}} - If {{content}} does not provide enough methodological detail for a subsection, note what information from the researcher would strengthen that subsection rather than inventing methods - Address NIH's emphasis on rigor and reproducibility throughout: * Scientific premise (addressed in Significance) * Scientific rigor in design * Consideration of sex as a biological variable * Authentication of key biological and chemical resources - Every methodology claim should be traceable to {{content}} or clearly labeled as proposed new methodology ALIGNMENT: - Read {{grant_info}} to determine the grant mechanism and scale the Approach accordingly: - R01 (5 years): Comprehensive approach with 2-4 aims, detailed methods, extensive preliminary data. Approach section typically 8-10 pages. - R21 (2 years): Focused exploratory approach with 1-2 aims, emphasis on feasibility. Approach section typically 3-4 pages. - R03 (2 years): Limited scope with 1-2 focused aims. Approach section typically 3-4 pages. - If {{grant_info}} specifies required methodological approaches, data types, or populations, ensure the Approach addresses those - If {{grant_info}} references specific NIH initiatives, common data elements, or required standards, incorporate those where they align with the researcher's methods NIH REVIEW CRITERIA TO ADDRESS: Approach: - Are the overall strategy, methodology, and analyses well-reasoned and appropriate to accomplish the specific aims? - Have the investigators presented strategies to ensure a robust and unbiased approach? - Are potential problems, alternative strategies, and benchmarks for success presented? - If the project is in the early stages, is the strategy reasonable given preliminary data? - Does the project involve clinical trials? If so, are design, conditions, and procedures appropriate? OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for "C. Approach" - Use

for "Preliminary Studies" and each Aim - Use

for subsections within each Aim (Rationale, Methods, Analysis, etc.) - Use

for sub-subsections where needed - Use

for paragraph content - Use

    /
  • for lists of potential problems and alternatives - Use , , , ,
    , for the timeline - Use for key terms sparingly - Scale length to grant mechanism: - R01: 8-10 pages (~5,000-6,000 words) - R21: 3-4 pages (~2,000-2,500 words) - R03: 3-4 pages (~2,000-2,500 words)
